title: Relentlessly focus on the outcomes
summary: AI makes it easy to focus inwards and feel impressed with all the churn and motion and output you're producing. But your users don't care.

title: Engineering levels aren't about skill anymore
summary: Traditional engineering levels were about skill progression. I think this has changed. Everyone now is a mini staff engineer.

title: Just fix their PR
summary: You know what's better than a code review comment? A fix.

title: "But if AI does it all, what's *my* job?"
summary: On AI, mental load, and delegation

title: What you need to make an agent
summary: I've been digging into agents for internal tooling and processes. Looking for how to scale without drowning in manual work. Here's what I found

title: Quick note on evals and putting AI in your resume
summary: When candidates put AI on their resume, the key thing I try to find out is whether they used evals. How did you measure making improvements?

title: AI now writes 97% of my code. Here's what I learned
summary: Forget the bullshit tips and tricks, just talk to it.

title: Just Read the Paper
summary: Read more papers. You can learn the latest and greatest in your field in one chill afternoon.

title: Watch people work
summary: We talk a lot about outcomes over outputs and how engineers are supposed to get us over the water, not blindly build a bridge because someone asked for a bridge. But how do you do that in practice?

title: Frustration Driven Development
summary: Frustration is your greatest engineering asset. What do you do the 5th time someone asks for the same damn thing yet again?

title: Software engineer interviews for the age of AI
summary: Maybe AI will replace engineers, I don't know. Self-driving cars were just around the corner for 50 years. Until then we've got shit to do and engineers to hire.

title: The best engineers just get shit done, sometimes with AI
summary: The best engineers I know never talk about their AI setup. The worst engineers I know only talk about their AI setup.

title: Taming chaos is a learnable skill
summary: How you approach software engineering makes it harder or easier to handle interruptions and other chaos. Writing a behavioral interview made me realize this is a learnable skill!

title: Put scope on your resume
summary: Your resume titles don't matter. Just tell me what did you do.

title: Probably the realest thing I ever wrote
Users buy your service, not your code
summary: You might enjoy this podcast episode. Sylvain and I talked about owning production in a world where AI writes the code.

title: GeLLMan amnesia
summary: AI will automate every job except the ones I'm deeply familiar with

title: The 3 curves that make a scalable business
summary: You want users and revenue to grow exponentially. Bugs per day to grow linearly. Support work to grow logarithmically. Your goal is to make this happen.

title: In praise of the stacked pull request
summary: How fast you do code review is how fast you do everything. Your PR queue is the leading indicator of team velocity. Stacked PRs can help.

title: How you migrate is everything
summary: Your code becomes legacy software when it works. You build something to solve a problem, then the problem changes. Or best practices evolve. Or you hire team members with more experience. Or the world changes. Or you have more people touching the same code and your beautiful architectural vision didn't translate.

title: The future of software engineering is SRE
summary: When code gets cheap operational excellence wins. Anyone can build a greenfield demo, but it takes engineering to run a service.

title: A tough job market is the best time to start something
summary: Here's how to navigate a tough job market from someone dumb enough to have done it before. Me.

title: Place good startup bets
summary: You can't win every bet. Your goal is to be a little more right than wrong and keep playing

title: Small projects, clear scope
summary: Minutes of planning save you days of programming. It is shocking how little planning it takes to make a big difference.

title: A better way to end-to-end test your webapp
summary: End-to-end tests are the best way to test your webapp, but they're flaky and annoying to write. I'm trying a new agentic approach that fixes the issue.

title: 2025 Year in Review
summary: I called it a year in review but it's so much more.

title: Are you playing games you want to win?
summary: As we enter the funny week-without-time between Christmas and New Year, it's a great time to reflect. Here's a framework I like to use.

title: How good engineering unlocks fast scaling
summary: You're building systems and tools to help companies grow without drowning in work. This fuels your equity, career capital, and reputation.

title: Scaling Fast – my new book on software engineering through the hockeystick
summary: After almost 3 years of writing, research, and editing Scaling Fast is now available in paperback and ebook wherever books are sold 🥳

title: What makes your resume stand out
summary: What did you do, for whom, did it work

title: How to lead a project
summary: you were asked to lead or own a project. Now what? Here are a few practical steps to help you succeed.

title: *People* detangle a ball of mud
summary: Ball of mud is the world's most popular software architecture. The one we all use at work. But it sucks to work with. So what do you do?

title: AI fills my day with busywork
summary: Remember when AI was gonna take our jobs? I've never felt busier. The more I get done, the more there is to do.

title: The unreasonable effectiveness of stack ranking
summary: Stakeholders are silly people. Everything feels important and urgent. Stack ranking helps

title: Chesterton's true lesson
summary: Too many engineers cry chestertons fence and never check why the code exists

title: The job behind the job [of a high level IC]
summary: Advancing big goals while you stay on top of operational day-to-day fires _is the job_. This is hard. Here are a few tips I've found helpful.

title: Make sure it works first
summary: Explore the importance of functionality over elegance in Swizec's latest piece. Learn from his experience of tripling an engineering team's size within a year.

title: Feedback is a strange thing
summary: Performance oscillates around your mean. Your average is what matters.

title: React, HTMX, and TanStack/Start
summary: More and more UI frameworks are ditching JSON as the transfer protocol and going back to server-rendered markup injected into your app

title: The grind won't get you there
summary: Don't grind on something the company doesn't value. Hard work is not rewarded. **_Outcomes_** are rewarded.

title: Most history is fake myth building
summary: Regimes come and go but the people stay. Observations from living through a socialism->capitalism transition

title: You may be looking for a useSyncExternalStore
summary: When you see a `useEffect` that updates a `useState` and returns a value, you might be looking for a `useSyncExternalStore`. This is my current vendetta.

title: How to be witty and off-the-cuff as a slow deep thinker
summary: Deep thinking is the super power of wit

title: Should you join an AI startup right now?
summary: Beyond income there's 2 reasons to take any specific job: Invest in yourself or invest in the company. Both is best.

title: Leadership lessons from growing 3x in 1 year
summary: So this talk is my attempt to step back and write down some of the things I've learned. Hope you find something to apply in your team.

title: Pair programming
summary: Great for learning those little tricks of the trade you forgot you even learned

title: Yes it's like spinning plates
summary: In a fast-growing company software engineering feels like spinning plates. Nothing ever stays fixed.

title: HTMX – Server Components without React
summary: Let's be honest: Half the APIs you write are for a specific purpose in a specific component on a specific page. Re-usable in theory but you've never tried. That's where Server Components come in ... but what if your server isn't JavaScript?

title: Quick tips for distributed event-based systems
summary: Hidden lessons painfully learned over the years.

title: Quick update on Scaling Fast, my next book
summary: Your goal is not to write a book, it's to change how your reader thinks

title: Code is the easy part, or how we refactored half the business to fix a janky script
summary: This is a classic startup war story: Someone had built a leaky rowboat. It worked! Then we started flying the rowboat as a Cessna. What we needed was an aircraft carrier.

title: Good software gets out of the way
summary: Explore the art of invisible software and user-centric workflow design with Swizec and Kent C Dodds. Uncover what separates a good engineer from a great one in this insightful dialogue.

title: You get what you hire
summary: Hiring engineers is like deciding what kind of system you'll have. This seems obvious but surprised me to see first-hand.

title: Using vibes to rewrite my blog
summary: The death of engineering has been greatly exaggerated. You still need to know what you're doing.

title: Theory of constraints, AI, and code review
summary: Feedback from an expert is how you learn taste. Your code doesn't matter until it's shipped.

title: The one thing every experienced engineer gets wrong
summary: In the last few months every engineer I hired stumbled in exactly the same way: Project management. They learned quickly, but that first big project has become a right of passage.
